assessment.summary: Documented AI harms are rising with adoption; the severe tail — misuse at scale or loss of control — is debated, but tracked and treated as a real risk by governments and intergovernmental bodies.
assessment.timeframe: Capabilities and adoption are advancing rapidly and documented incidents are rising; expert estimates of severe global outcomes span a very wide range.

claims.text: The total number of AI incidents and hazards reported in public media surged between 2022 and 2025, per OECD analysis of the OECD.AI Incidents and Hazards Monitor (AIM).

Media-reported AI incidents and hazards linked to synthetic media, child safety, and cyberattacks and fraud have more than doubled since 2022 (OECD Artificial Intelligence Papers No. 53, February 2026).

NIST released the AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) on 26 January 2023 to help manage risks to individuals, organizations, and society associated with artificial intelligence, and released a Generative AI Profile (NIST-AI-600-1) on 26 July 2024.

published.description: Risks from increasingly capable and widely deployed AI systems: documented present-day harms (synthetic media, fraud, cyberattacks, child-safety incidents), plus contested longer-term scenarios of large-scale misuse or loss of control. Governments and standards bodies now track incidents and publish risk-management frameworks, but quantified estimates of the severe tail remain scarce by nature.
published.name: Advanced AI Risk

assessment.summary: A present, quantified global strain — hundreds of millions hungry, billions water-insecure — whose escalation pathways run through climate extremes and conflict.
assessment.timeframe: Chronic and ongoing; acute regional food crises recur annually, and climate change intensifies water stress through mid-century.

claims.text: An estimated 8.2 percent of the global population, about 673 million people, experienced hunger in 2024 (FAO, The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the World 2025).
The global prevalence of moderate or severe food insecurity was 28.0 percent in 2024, accounting for 2.3 billion people (FAO SOFI 2025).
266 million people are facing crisis levels of hunger or worse, according to the Global Report on Food Crises.
Roughly half of the world's population experiences severe water scarcity for at least part of the year (UN World Water Development Report 2024).
As of 2022, 2.2 billion people were without access to safely managed drinking water (UN World Water Development Report 2024).

published.description: A chronic global shortfall in access to adequate food and safe freshwater, driven by conflict, economic shocks, and climate extremes. Hundreds of millions face hunger and billions face water scarcity today; the tail risk is cascading food-system and water-supply failures that destabilize whole regions.
published.name: Global Food and Water Insecurity

assessment.summary: Over a hundred million people remain forcibly displaced worldwide — a standing stress on regional stability and the international protection system.
assessment.timeframe: Ongoing at historic scale; direction depends on active conflicts, with returns in 2025 producing the first decline in ten years.

claims.text: At the end of 2025, 117.8 million people worldwide were forcibly displaced — roughly one in every 70 people (UNHCR Global Trends, June 2026).
Global refugee numbers fell by three per cent in 2025 to 41.6 million.
14.7 million displaced people went back to their areas or countries of origin in 2025, including 4.4 million refugees and 10.3 million internally displaced people.
70 per cent of refugees are living in protracted, long-term displacement situations (UNHCR Global Trends briefing, June 2026).

published.description: Displacement by persecution, conflict, violence, and human-rights violations at near-record scale. Sustained mass displacement strains host states and international institutions, and is both a symptom and an amplifier of instability; 2025 saw the first decline in a decade, driven by large-scale returns.
published.name: Global Forced Displacement Crisis

assessment.summary: An ongoing, human-caused warming of the climate system with civilizational-scale risks under continued high emissions.
assessment.timeframe: Already underway; impacts intensify with cumulative emissions.

claims.text: The IPCC's Sixth Assessment Report attributes approximately 1.1 °C of global warming since 1850-1900 to human activities.

published.description: Sustained warming of Earth's climate system driven by human greenhouse-gas emissions, with widespread impacts on weather extremes, sea level, ecosystems, and human systems. Unlike discrete events, it is an ongoing, intensifying process.
published.name: Climate Change (Global Warming)

assessment.summary: A recurrent biological threat whose severe-pandemic form could greatly exceed the already-large annual burden of seasonal influenza.
assessment.timeframe: Pandemics recur on the order of every few decades; timing is not predictable.

claims.text: The WHO estimates that seasonal influenza causes about 290,000 to 650,000 respiratory deaths worldwide each year.
The WHO estimates that annual seasonal influenza epidemics result in about 3 to 5 million cases of severe illness worldwide.

published.description: A novel influenza strain to which the population has little immunity could spread worldwide and cause mortality far exceeding ordinary seasonal influenza, as in 1918. Influenza pandemics have recurred several times per century.
published.name: Influenza Pandemic

assessment.summary: A rare, high-consequence impact; no known large object currently threatens Earth, though the catalogue of hazardous-size objects is incomplete.
assessment.timeframe: No known object of concern is on an impact trajectory this century.

claims.text: NASA reports that no known asteroid larger than 140 meters has a significant chance of hitting Earth for the next 100 years.
NASA reports that fewer than half of the estimated 25,000 near-Earth objects 140 meters and larger have been found to date.

published.description: A sufficiently large near-Earth object striking Earth would cause regional to continental devastation. NASA's Planetary Defense effort catalogues and tracks the larger near-Earth object population to assess impact hazards decades in advance.
published.name: Near-Earth Asteroid Impact

assessment.summary: Thousands of warheads remain in military stockpiles — thousands deployed, and roughly two thousand on high alert — making nuclear war a persistent, low-probability, civilization-scale risk.
assessment.timeframe: No reliable forecast; risk tracks geopolitical crises, arsenal modernization, and the erosion or renewal of arms-control agreements.

claims.text: SIPRI estimates a total global inventory of 12,187 nuclear warheads in January 2026, of which about 9,745 were in military stockpiles for potential use.

An estimated 4,012 warheads were deployed with missiles and aircraft in January 2026, and between 2,100 and 2,200 of the deployed warheads were kept in a state of high operational alert on ballistic missiles.
Nine states possess nuclear weapons: the United States, Russia, the United Kingdom, France, China, India, Pakistan, North Korea and Israel.

published.description: A deliberate or inadvertent nuclear exchange between states. Global arsenals remain in the thousands of warheads, all nine nuclear-armed states are modernizing their forces, and hundreds of weapons are kept on high operational alert — keeping a civilization-scale exchange a persistent possibility even at low annual probability.
published.name: Nuclear War

assessment.summary: A continent-scale disaster with extremely low annual probability and no current signs of imminent eruption.
assessment.timeframe: No reliable short-term forecast. The three largest past eruptions occurred ~2.1M, ~1.3M, and ~0.64M years ago.

claims.text: The USGS estimates the annual probability of a Yellowstone supereruption at roughly 1 in 730,000.
Yellowstone has produced three caldera-forming eruptions in the past ~2.1 million years.

published.description: A large caldera system beneath Yellowstone National Park capable of a 'supereruption' that would blanket much of North America in ash and inject climate-altering aerosols into the stratosphere. Such eruptions are extraordinarily rare and there is no evidence one is imminent.
published.name: Yellowstone Supervolcano
